these are the only possibilities, and become confused when they meet sentences These are useful structures to practise. However, students sometimes think that If we had played tennis, I would have won.

if + past perfect            would have + past participle 'third conditional’ If we played tennis,   I would win.

if + past                          would + infinitive 'second conditional’ If we play tennis,    I'll win.

if + present                 will + infinitive 'first conditional’ with if, which  are often called the 'first’ , 'second' and 'third’  conditionals.

Some students’  grammars concentrate on three common sentence structures 'first  ', 'second'     and   'third'   conditionals;         other     structures Clauses of this kind are often called ‘ conditional’  clauses.

Oil floats if you pour it on water.

If you get here before eight, we can catch the early train.

something else can happen.

An if-clause often refers to a condition - something which  must happen so that conditions If I see Annie, I'll give her your love. (I may or may not see Annie.) Ask Jack if he's staying tonight. (He may or may not be staying.) which  may or may not happen, which  may or may not be true, etc.
